content.text: An API wrapper tool gives a model the ability to query or mutate an external service — a weather API, a database, a payment processor, a GitHub repository. The model decides when to call the tool and what arguments to pass; the handler executes the actual HTTP request, injects credentials, parses the response, and returns a structured result the model can act on. The model never sees the API key or the raw HTTP details.

attrs.alt: API wrapper tool request/response flow from model through handler to external API
attrs.title: API Wrapper Tool Flow

content.text: The tool schema is a contract between your code and the model. The handler is a contract between your code and the external API. Keep them separate: the schema describes what the model must provide; the handler handles everything the model should not see.

The schema exposes only the parameters the model needs to specify — identifiers, search terms, filters, and pagination tokens. Authentication parameters never appear in the schema. Include a clear description of what each field accepts and omit any optional parameter that has a sensible default the handler can apply.
The Handler: Auth, Request, Parse, Return

The handler does five things in sequence: validate inputs, inject credentials from environment variables, make the HTTP request with a timeout, parse the response into a flat structured dict, and return either the result or a structured error.

Raw API responses are often large — a GitHub issue object has over 30 fields, most irrelevant to the model's next decision. Returning the full object wastes tokens and can confuse the model with noise. Select only the fields the model needs and give them stable, predictable names.

# Full GitHub issue API response contains these fields (partial list):
# id, node_id, url, repository_url, labels_url, comments_url,
# events_url, html_url, number, state, title, user, labels,
# assignee, assignees, milestone, locked, active_lock_reason,
# comments, pull_request, closed_at, created_at, updated_at,
# author_association, body, reactions, timeline_url, ...

# Pruned to what the model needs:
def _prune_issue(item: dict) -> dict:
    return {
        "id": item["number"],
        "title": item["title"],
        "url": item["html_url"],
        "labels": [lb["name"] for lb in item["labels"]],
        "comments": item["comments"],
        "created_at": item["created_at"][:10],  # date only, not full ISO timestamp
    }

When an API returns paginated results, expose a page_token or page parameter in the schema so the model can request subsequent pages. Include has_more and next_page_token in every response so the model knows whether to keep paginating.

def list_pull_requests(
    repo: str,
    state: str = "open",
    page: int = 1,
    per_page: int = 20,
) -> dict:
    resp = _SESSION.get(
        f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/pulls",
        params={"state": state, "page": page, "per_page": per_page},
        timeout=10,
    )
    if not resp.ok:
        return {
            "error": "SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE",
            "message": f"GitHub returned HTTP {resp.status_code}.",
            "retryable": True,
        }
    items = resp.json()
    # GitHub uses Link header to indicate more pages
    has_more = "next" in resp.links
    return {
        "results": [{"id": pr["number"], "title": pr["title"], "url": pr["html_url"]} for pr in items],
        "page": page,
        "per_page": per_page,
        "has_more": has_more,
        "next_page": page + 1 if has_more else None,
    }

Write operations follow the same pattern but need extra care: validate inputs before making the request (to avoid a round-trip for obviously bad args), return the created or updated resource so the model has its id, and use a clear CONFLICT error when a duplicate exists.
Using the Tool in an Agentic Loop
Register the tool schema in the tools list and route tool_use blocks by name to the corresponding handler function. The same safe_tool_handler pattern from the Tool Error Messages topic applies here.

Test handlers in isolation by patching the HTTP session. Verify the schema maps correctly to the handler signature, that error paths return structured errors, and that the pruned output contains exactly the fields you expect.

from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch
import pytest

def test_search_returns_pruned_fields():
    mock_resp = MagicMock()
    mock_resp.ok = True
    mock_resp.status_code = 200
    mock_resp.json.return_value = {
        "total_count": 1,
        "items": [{
            "number": 42,
            "title": "Rate limiting docs unclear",
            "html_url": "https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-sdk-python/issues/42",
            "labels": [{"name": "documentation"}],
            "comments": 3,
            "created_at": "2024-11-01T10:00:00Z",
            # many other fields that should NOT appear in output
            "body": "...", "state": "open", "user": {"login": "alice"},
        }],
    }
    with patch.object(_SESSION, "get", return_value=mock_resp):
        result = search_github_issues("anthropics/anthropic-sdk-python", "rate limit")
    assert result["total_count"] == 1
    issue = result["results"][0]
    assert set(issue.keys()) == {"id", "title", "url", "labels", "comments", "created_at"}
    assert "body" not in issue

def test_timeout_returns_retryable_error():
    with patch.object(_SESSION, "get", side_effect=requests.Timeout):
        result = search_github_issues("anthropics/anthropic-sdk-python", "rate limit")
    assert result["error"] == "SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE"
    assert result["retryable"] is True

Creating a new requests.Session per tool call is expensive — it opens a new TCP connection and re-sends headers every time. A module-level session with connection pooling reuses connections across calls in the same process, cutting latency by 30–60 ms per call on keep-alive endpoints.

import os
import requests
from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter
from urllib3.util.retry import Retry

def _build_session(api_key: str) -> requests.Session:
    session = requests.Session()
    session.headers.update({
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "User-Agent": "my-agent/1.0",
    })
    # Retry on transient network errors (not on 4xx — those are caller errors)
    retry = Retry(
        total=3,
        backoff_factor=0.5,
        status_forcelist=[502, 503, 504],
        allowed_methods=["GET"],  # never auto-retry POST
    )
    adapter = HTTPAdapter(max_retries=retry)
    session.mount("https://", adapter)
    return session

# Module-level singleton — reused across all calls in this process
_SESSION = _build_session(os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"])
Set allowed_methods=["GET"] on the retry adapter. Automatically retrying POST requests risks creating duplicate resources if the first request succeeded but the response was lost in transit.
Comparing REST, GraphQL, and gRPC Wrappers

REST and GraphQL handlers follow the pattern shown above. For gRPC, create a channel and stub at module level instead of a Session, and catch grpc.RpcError to map status codes to your error vocabulary.

Some API calls return data that changes rarely — repository metadata, user profiles, product catalogues. Caching these in a simple in-process dict avoids redundant network round-trips when the model calls the tool multiple times in one agentic session.

import time
from typing import Any

_CACHE: dict[str, tuple[float, Any]] = {}  # key -> (expires_at, value)
_TTL = 300  # 5 minutes

def get_repo_info(repo: str) -> dict:
    cache_key = f"repo:{repo}"
    now = time.monotonic()
    if cache_key in _CACHE:
        expires_at, cached = _CACHE[cache_key]
        if now < expires_at:
            return cached

    resp = _SESSION.get(
        f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}",
        timeout=10,
    )
    if not resp.ok:
        return {"error": "NOT_FOUND", "message": f"Repo {repo!r} not found.", "retryable": False}
    data = resp.json()
    result = {
        "name": data["full_name"],
        "description": data["description"],
        "stars": data["stargazers_count"],
        "open_issues": data["open_issues_count"],
        "default_branch": data["default_branch"],
    }
    _CACHE[cache_key] = (now + _TTL, result)
    return result

Use time.monotonic() for cache expiry, not time.time(), to avoid issues with system clock adjustments. For production deployments with multiple processes, replace the in-process dict with a shared Redis cache.
An API wrapper tool's schema exposes only model-facing parameters — credentials and session state live in the handler.
The handler is responsible for auth injection, timeout enforcement, HTTP status mapping, response pruning, and structured error returns.
Prune API responses to the minimum fields the model needs for its next decision — every extra field costs tokens on the next turn.
Return structured errors with error, message, and retryable; map HTTP 403 → PERMISSION_DENIED, 404 → NOT_FOUND, 422 → VALIDATION_ERROR, 429/503 → retryable errors.
For paginated APIs, include has_more and next_page in every list response so the model can iterate without guessing.
Test handlers in isolation by patching the HTTP session — verify pruned field set, all error paths, and timeout behaviour.
Use a single stateful requests.Session with the auth header set once at startup, not re-created per call.
